                                                                                      Sampling error. The standard error is primarily a measure of sampl-
         There are two types of errors possible in an estimate based on a          ing variability, that is, of the variation that occurs by chance because a
      sample survey—sampling and nonsampling. The standard errors pro-             sample rather than the entire population is surveyed. The sample
      vided primarily indicate the magnitude of the sampling error. They           estimate and its estimated standard error enable one to construct con-
      also partially measure the effect of some nonsampling errors in              fidence intervals, ranges that would include the average of all possible
      response and enumeration but do not measure any systematic biases in         samples with a known probability. For example, if all possible
      the data.                                                                    samples were selected, each of these surveyed under essentially the
         Nonsampling errors. The full extent of nonsampling error is               same general conditions and using the sample sample design, and an
      unknown, but special studies have been conducted to quantify some            estimate and its estimated error were calculated from each sample,

        One measure of the reliability of the employment estimates for in-       Table Q. Errors of preliminary employment estimates
      dividual industries is the root-mean-square error (RMSE). The
      measure is the standard deviation adjusted for the bias in estimates:                                                     Root- mean- square
                                                                                                                                   error of—
          RMSE =      V(Standard Deviation)2 + (Bias)2
                                                                                                 Category                                   Month-to-
                                                                                                                              Monthly
      If the bias is small, the chances are about 2 out of 3 that an estimate                                                                month
                                                                                                                               level
      from the sample would differ from its benchmark by less than the                                                                       change
      root-mean-square error. The chances are about 19 out of 20 that the
      difference would be less than twice the root-mean-square error.

                                                      Seasonal adjustment

           Over a course of a year, the size of the Nation's labor force, the          All civilian labor force and unemployment rate statistics, as well as
       evels of employment and unemployment, and other measures of                  the major employment and unemployment estimates, are computed
       abor market activity undergo sharp fluctuations due to such seasonal         by aggregating independently adjusted series. For example, for each
       vents as changes in weather, reduced or expanded production,                 of the three major labor force components—agricultural employment,
       larvests, major holidays, and the opening and closing of schools,            nonagricultural employment, and unemployment—data for four sex-
       because these seasonal events follow a more or less regular pattern          age groups (males and females under and over 20 years of age) are
       :ach year, their influence on statistical trends can be eliminated by ad-    separately adjusted for seasonal variation and are then added to derive
       usting the statistics from month to month. These adjustments make it         seasonally adjusted total figures. In order to provide seasonally ad-
